narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 61% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have some solid strengths, like ranking in the 7th decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—better than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile position—along with a strong LSAT score of 39, an undergraduate GPA of 3.40, and coming from an upper-middle-class family background. As a white female planning to attend full-time, you align closely with the most common profiles in our data, where full-time students make up over 90% and white students about 84%. Despite these positives, the model focused most heavily on your third-year school decile as the top factor nudging the prediction toward failure, even though your rank was above average. Your first-year decile came in second, also slightly pushing the odds toward a fail prediction. Family income ranked third in influence, contributing in the same direction, followed closely by your LSAT score and racial background, both adding a bit more weight toward the failure side. Your undergraduate GPA was next, with a similar effect, while your gender as female and full-time status provided the smallest counterbalances, gently pulling toward a pass prediction. In essence, the model saw your academic deciles as the strongest signals, outweighing your test scores and background in this case. Compared to typical students who passed, your profile is strong in raw metrics but may reflect patterns where mid-to-upper deciles didn't always translate to bar success for similar applicants. To boost your future chances, focus on honing the study habits and skills that drive higher academic deciles, as those emerged as the most changeable predictors here—perhaps through targeted prep courses or tutoring before retaking any key exams. Building even stronger performance in school-like settings could shift the model's view in your favor next time. You're already ahead in several areas, so with some refinement, a better outcome is within reach. Hang in there; many students turn predictions around with focused effort.
